narrative:
Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which evaluates applicants based on their likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try, estimated a 64% chance that you would not pass. This prediction draws from patterns in data about thousands of past students, including their grades, test scores, family backgrounds, and more, to help the school make informed admission decisions. For you, as a Black male applicant, the model zeroed in on a few key aspects of your profile that shaped this outcome.

The most influential factor pushing toward this prediction of failure was your LSAT score of 22, which is notably lower than the average of about 37 across past students. This stood out as the strongest signal to the model, suggesting a higher risk of struggling on the bar exam compared to applicants with stronger scores. In contrast, your family's upper-middle income bracket worked in your favor, pulling the prediction slightly toward success since students from similar backgrounds have historically performed better.

Your Year 1 grade decile of 3—below the typical 5.7 average—also nudged things positively in a smaller way, indicating some early academic resilience that the model viewed helpfully, though not enough to offset the bigger concerns. Overall, your undergraduate GPA of 2.2 (versus the 3.21 average), Year 3 decile of 4, and full-time student status played supporting roles but weren't the main drivers here. Compared to typical admitted students, who tend to have higher test scores and GPAs aligning closer to or above these averages, your profile signaled elevated risk to the model.

While this is disappointing, especially given your background and determination to pursue law, it's not the end of the road. Consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, as that's the top lever you can pull to shift future predictions in your favor. Strengthening your academic record through additional coursework or targeting schools with more holistic reviews could also open doors, and many successful lawyers have overcome similar early hurdles by focusing on targeted preparation for the bar itself.
